ratings.plot: The former famous painter Frenhofer revisits an abandoned project using the girlfriend of a young visiting artist. Questions about truth, life, and artistic limits are explored.

verdict.reason: Worth the trip if you can commit to four hours of Rivette at his most uncompromising. The camera holds on every brushstroke in real time, turning the act of painting into pure cinema.

ratings.plot: A widow tries to marry off her daughter with the help of her late husband's three friends.

verdict.reason: Essential Ozu that belongs on a big screen with proper projection. His camera placement and cutting rhythms create a visual architecture that streaming cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: A guide leads two men through an area known as the Zone to find a room that grants wishes.

verdict.reason: See it theatrically while you can—this is Tarkovsky's most immersive film. The sound design and long takes demand the focus that only a darkened theater provides.

ratings.plot: A spell of time of a rural family's slightly surreal life.

verdict.reason: Charming but minor Ishii that works better as comfort viewing than essential cinema. The whimsical tone stretches thin over nearly two and a half hours.

ratings.plot: In Paris outskirts Blanche, a young clerk, befriends Lea, a girl livelier than she is. Lea is going steady with Fabien who is a friend to Alexandre who is going steady with Adrienne but is however loved by Blanche. Somehow a way h...

verdict.reason: Peak Rohmer that captures the entire Moral Tales project in microcosm. The dialogue rhythms and behavioral precision reward theatrical attention.

ratings.plot: This biopic of Norwegian Expressionist painter Edvard Munch focuses on the influences that shaped his art, his devastating affair with a married woman that will haunt him for the rest of his life

verdict.reason: Watkins' experimental approach creates a unique biographical form worth seeing once. The three-and-a-half-hour runtime tests even devoted cinephiles.

ratings.plot: A young French girl orphaned in a German air attack is befriended by the son of a poor farmer, and together they try to come to terms with the realities of death.

verdict.reason: Essential French cinema that earns its reputation through Clément's unsentimental direction. The child performances anchor a war film that never exploits its tragedy.

movies.title: Four Adventures of Reinette and Mirabelle

ratings.plot: Two unlike girls are going to learn each other's world - lovely countryside and bustling Paris.

verdict.reason: Rohmer's most accessible film still rewards close attention to his moral universe. The episodic structure builds to surprising emotional weight.

ratings.plot: A young boy and his little sister struggle to survive in Japan during World War II.

verdict.reason: Takahata's animation deserves the biggest screen possible—this is not Netflix comfort food. The hand-drawn imagery achieves a devastating beauty that demands theatrical viewing.

ratings.plot: Utamaro is a great artist who lives to create portraits of beautiful women, using the brothels of Tokyo to provide his models.

verdict.reason: Essential Mizoguchi showcasing his mastery of camera movement and composition. The brothel setting allows for some of cinema's greatest long takes.

ratings.plot: Eight-year-old Ahmed has mistakenly taken his friend Mohammad's notebook. He wants to return it, or else his friend will be expelled from school. The boy determinedly sets out to find Mohammad's home in the neighbouring village.

verdict.reason: Kiarostami's breakthrough transforms a simple premise into profound cinema. The Iranian landscape and natural light create images that lose impact on small screens.

ratings.plot: N/A

verdict.reason: Aljafari's latest work likely demands theatrical viewing for full impact. Without more information about the specific film, the director's reputation suggests experimental documentary worth seeing once.

Essential viewing for Jia Zhangke's breakthrough debut that launched sixth-generation Chinese cinema. The handheld DV photography captures working-class displacement with an intimacy that established his entire aesthetic approach.

ratings.plot: Tales told from the perspective of a lonely old tree standing in the middle of a botanical garden.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Enyedi's return after her Golden Bear triumph with On Body and Soul. The 147-minute runtime lets her poetic vision breathe without rushing the tree's centuries-spanning observations.

ratings.plot: Two wealthy law-school students go on trial for murder in this version of the Leopold-Loeb case.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Fleischer's clinical direction turning a famous case into courtroom procedural gold. Orson Welles delivers one of his finest supporting performances as the defense attorney in the film's explosive final act.

ratings.plot: A woman planning to testify against the mob must be protected against potential assassins on the train trip from Chicago to Los Angeles.

verdict.reason: Essential noir that earns its reputation as one of the tightest thrillers ever made. Fleischer transforms a single train into a masterclass of spatial tension where every car becomes a potential death trap.

ratings.plot: John Davidson: diagnosed with Tourette's syndrome at a young age which alienated him from his peers, he struggled with a condition few people had witnessed.

verdict.reason: See this powerful biographical drama while it has theatrical momentum. Jones directs with restraint that lets the lead performance carry the emotional weight without exploitation.

ratings.plot: The trials and tribulations of Eddie and other transvestites in Japan.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of Japanese cinema's most radical experiments with narrative structure and sexual identity. Matsumoto's fractured editing and documentary-fiction hybrid approach creates a viewing experience that remains genuinely shocking and innovative 50 years later.

ratings.plot: A family of six settles into their new home on Vancouver Island as internal dynamics are slowly revealed through the eyes of the youngest child.

verdict.reason: See this intimate family portrait while it has theatrical momentum. Romvari shoots through a child's perspective with uncommon sensitivity to how kids actually process adult tensions.

ratings.plot: A bored photographer recovering from a broken leg passes the time by watching his neighbors and begins to suspect one of them of murder.

verdict.reason: Essential Hitchcock that demands the big screen experience. The film's entire architecture depends on you sharing Stewart's confined viewpoint, which only works in a theater.

ratings.plot: Pulp novelist Holly Martins travels to shadowy, postwar Vienna, only to find himself investigating the mysterious death of an old friend, Harry Lime.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing on the big screen where Reed's tilted camera angles and deep focus photography can work their full magic. The zither music and Vienna's bombed-out sewers create an atmosphere that streaming cannot match.

ratings.plot: A comedy-drama about a bereaved mother, an overwrought actress, her jealous lover, and a pregnant nun.

verdict.reason: Essential Almodóvar at his most emotionally generous and theatrically alive. The director's command of melodrama and his ensemble of women deliver one of cinema's great portraits of resilience.

ratings.plot: A doomed female hitchhiker pulls Mike Hammer into a deadly whirlpool of intrigue, revolving around a mysterious "great whatsit".

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of the most violent and apocalyptic noir films ever made. Aldrich turns Mickey Spillane's pulp into a nuclear-age nightmare that ends with literal atomic fire.

ratings.plot: After being fired, a man devises a unique plan to secure a new job: eliminate his competition.

verdict.reason: Park Chan-wook's return to dark comedy territory delivers his signature blend of surgical precision and moral chaos. The 139-minute runtime gives him space to build the kind of intricate revenge machinery that made Oldboy essential viewing.

movies.title: Portrait of a Lady on Fire

ratings.plot: On an isolated island in Brittany at the end of the eighteenth century, a female painter is obliged to paint a wedding portrait of a young woman.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone who believes cinema can capture the intensity of unspoken desire. Sciamma builds every frame around the act of looking, making the theater the perfect place to experience her controlled visual intimacy.

ratings.plot: A journey over the walls that divide us, to explore the age-old debate of land and power, amid the fight for greater access to nature.

verdict.reason: Strong documentary filmmaking but lacks the cinematic scope that demands big screen viewing. Wallace delivers solid journalism about land access issues that works just as well at home.

ratings.plot: A surgeon causes a car accident which leaves his daughter disfigured and goes to extreme lengths to give her a new face.

verdict.reason: Franju's horror masterpiece belongs on the biggest screen you can find. The black and white cinematography creates shadows and textures that streaming compression destroys.

ratings.plot: After suffering a near-fatal head injury, a young cowboy undertakes a search for new identity and what it means to be a man in the heartland of America.

verdict.reason: Zhao captures the American West with an authenticity that makes this essential theater viewing. The rodeo sequences and Dakota landscapes lose their power on anything smaller than a cinema screen.

ratings.plot: The life, times and afflictions of the fifteenth-century Russian iconographer St. Andrei Rublev.

verdict.reason: Tarkovsky's medieval epic rewards patience but punishes casual viewing with its three-hour runtime. The bell-casting finale alone justifies the theatrical experience if you can commit fully.

ratings.plot: A mother desperately searches for the killer who framed her son for a girl's horrific murder.

verdict.reason: Bong Joon Ho's thriller builds suspense through precise sound design and framing that streaming flattens. Kim Hye-ja's powerhouse performance demands the focused attention only a theater provides.

ratings.plot: In London, four very different people team up on a jewel heist, then try to double-cross one another for the loot, complicated by their efforts to fool the very proper barrister Archibald Leach.

verdict.reason: Cleese and Crichton's comedy holds up but gains nothing essential from theatrical viewing. The ensemble cast delivers laughs that work just as well on your couch.

ratings.plot: This Hong Kong-set crime drama follows the lives of a hitman, hoping to get out of the business, and his elusive female partner.

verdict.reason: Wong Kar-wai's neon-soaked Hong Kong comes alive on a big screen where you can catch every detail of his restless camera. The film's kinetic energy gets lost on smaller formats.

ratings.plot: In 1930s Korea, a girl is hired as a handmaiden to a Japanese heiress who lives a secluded life on a countryside estate. But the maid has a secret: She is a pickpocket recruited by a swindler to help seduce the Lady and steal her ...

verdict.reason: Park Chan-wook's intricate thriller rewards the big screen treatment with its layered production design. The film's multiple reveals benefit from the immersive focus that only theatrical viewing provides.

ratings.plot: After breaking the mysterious "One Wish Willow" to win his crush's heart, a hopeless romantic finds himself getting exactly what he asked for but soon discovers that some desires come at a dark, sinister price.

verdict.reason: Generic horror premise dressed up with high production values that cannot mask thin storytelling. Barker's direction lacks the innovation needed to elevate familiar wish-fulfillment scares.

ratings.plot: Every night a shepherd reads aloud a murder mystery, pretending his sheep can understand. When he is found dead, the sheep realize at once that it was a murder and think they know everything about how to go about solving it.

verdict.reason: Illumination-style animation wrapped around a premise too cute for its own good. Balda's direction feels assembly-line despite the quirky murder mystery angle.

ratings.plot: The story involves Maverick confronting his past while training a group of younger Top Gun graduates, including the son of his deceased best friend, for a dangerous mission.

verdict.reason: Skip the streaming version and see this in theaters where it belongs. Kosinski shoots the aerial sequences with practical effects that make IMAX screens feel like cockpit windows.

ratings.plot: Follows a donkey who encounters on his journeys good and bad people, experiences joy and pain, exploring a vision of modern Europe through his eyes.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Skolimowski's haunting vision of modern cruelty and compassion. The donkey protagonist creates an unexpectedly powerful emotional experience that works only in a theater.

ratings.plot: Meet a mother and daughter, high-society dropouts, reclusive cousins of Jackie O., managing to thrive together amid the decay and disorder of their East Hampton, NY, mansion, making for an eerily ramshackle echo of the American Ca...
ratings.director: Ellen Hovde, Albert Maysles, David Maysles

verdict.reason: One of the greatest documentaries ever made deserves the big screen treatment. The Maysles brothers capture intimate decay with a cinematic eye that rewards theatrical viewing.

ratings.plot: Returning to a hotel now haunted by its mysterious past, an artist and her elderly mother confront long-buried secrets in their former family home.

verdict.reason: Hogg's ghost story rewards patient viewers but moves at her characteristic glacial pace. Tilda Swinton's dual performance anchors an otherwise deliberately opaque narrative.

ratings.plot: After her werewolf lover unexpectedly dies in an accident, a young woman must find ways to raise their werewolf son and daughter while keeping their trait hidden from society.

verdict.reason: Hosoda's animation achieves rare emotional depth that benefits from theatrical immersion. The hand-drawn sequences of rural life contain visual poetry that streaming cannot match.

movies.title: Jeanne Dielman 23 quai du commerce 1080 Bruxelles

ratings.plot: A lonely widowed housewife does her daily chores, takes care of her apartment where she lives with her teenage son, and turns the occasional trick to make ends meet, but something happens that changes her safe routine.

verdict.reason: Akerman's masterpiece demands commitment to over three hours of real-time domestic routine. The film's power builds through accumulated detail that tests even devoted cinephiles.

ratings.plot: A supermom lands a Shark Tank deal, switching roles with her breadwinner husband. He struggles to adapt as a stay-at-home dad to their three daughters.

verdict.reason: Generic role-reversal comedy relies on tired gender stereotypes without fresh insight. Eric Appel brings no distinctive voice to well-worn family dysfunction material.

ratings.plot: The Story of Everything takes us on a journey into the origins of humanity and the cosmos, illuminating mankind's most important scientific discoveries and revealing that everything has been created by a designer.

verdict.reason: Creationist documentary disguised as science undermines genuine cosmological wonder. The film's predetermined conclusions make for dishonest inquiry into legitimate scientific questions.

ratings.plot: Nora and Hae Sung, two deeply connected childhood friends, are wrested apart after Nora's family emigrates from South Korea. Twenty years later, they are reunited for one fateful week as they confront notions of love and destiny.

verdict.reason: Celine Song's debut captures romantic longing with remarkable restraint and visual sophistication. The film's quiet power builds to an ending that justifies the theatrical experience.
Insufficient information available to make a strong recommendation either way. Without knowing the director, runtime, or basic premise, the theatrical value remains unclear.

ratings.plot: A detective investigating a man's death in the mountains meets the dead man's mysterious wife in the course of his dogged sleuthing.

verdict.reason: Park Chan-wook delivers his most elegant thriller with mystery that rewards close attention. The film's visual language and shifting perspectives demand the focus that only theaters provide.

ratings.plot: A science teacher wakes up alone on a spaceship. As his memory returns, he uncovers a mission to stop a mysterious substance killing Earth's sun and that an unexpected friendship may be the key.

verdict.reason: Lord and Miller's space adventure could deliver crowd-pleasing entertainment but risks becoming another generic blockbuster. The 156-minute runtime suggests bloated spectacle over tight storytelling.

ratings.plot: Carrie White, a shy, friendless teenage girl who is sheltered by her domineering, religious mother, unleashes her telekinetic powers after being humiliated by her classmates at her senior prom.

verdict.reason: De Palma's horror masterpiece belongs on the biggest screen possible for maximum impact. The prom sequence remains one of cinema's great set pieces that loses power on smaller formats.

ratings.plot: Follows a group of shoplifters who take aim at a cutthroat fashion maven.

verdict.reason: Riley delivers his sharpest social satire yet with perfect tonal control. The fashion industry backdrop gives him fresh material to skewer capitalism with his signature wit.

ratings.plot: Ten years after her aunt was murdered in their London home, a woman returns from Italy in the 1880s to resume residence with her new husband. His obsessive interest in the house rises from a secret that may require driving his wif...

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Cukor's masterful direction of psychological terror in a theatrical setting. Ingrid Bergman's performance as a woman questioning her own sanity is one of the greatest in cinema history.

ratings.plot: The story of Oscar Grant III, a 22-year-old Bay Area resident, who crosses paths with friends, enemies, family, and strangers on the last day of 2008.

Private eye Philip Marlowe helps friend Terry Lennox out of a jam and is implicated in his wife Sylvia's murder. He also is hired by Eileen Wade to locate her dipsomaniac husband Roger, who frequently disappears when he wants to d...

verdict.reason: Essential Altman that belongs on the big screen where his overlapping dialogue and wide compositions can breathe. Gould's mumbling, chain-smoking Marlowe is the perfect vehicle for the director's cynical demolition of genre conventions.

ratings.plot: Cleo, a singer and hypochondriac, becomes increasingly worried that she might have cancer while awaiting test results from her doctor.

verdict.reason: Essential Varda that captures real-time anxiety better than almost any film ever made. The 90-minute runtime unfolds in near real-time as Cléo wanders Paris, creating an intimacy that demands theatrical focus.
Essential viewing for one of cinema's greatest action sequences. Miller's practical effects and stunt work remain unmatched decades later.

ratings.plot: Two 12-year-olds, who live on an island, fall in love with each other and elope into the wilderness. While people set out on a search mission, a violent storm approaching them catches their attention.

verdict.reason: Anderson's most emotionally direct film deserves the big screen treatment. The 16mm cinematography captures New England's golden hour magic in ways that streaming cannot match.

ratings.plot: Violence and mayhem ensue after a hunter stumbles upon the aftermath of a drug deal gone wrong and over two million dollars in cash near the Rio Grande.

verdict.reason: Essential Coen Brothers filmmaking that demands the big screen experience. Javier Bardem's chilling performance as Anton Chigurh creates unbearable tension that works best in a darkened theater.

ratings.plot: A suburban housewife's world falls apart when she finds that her pornographer husband is serially unfaithful to her, her daughter is pregnant, and her son is suspected of being the foot-fetishist who's been breaking local women's ...

verdict.reason: Essential Waters at his most accessible without losing his transgressive edge. The Odorama gimmick turns a theatrical screening into a proper event you cannot replicate at home.

ratings.plot: A man becomes increasingly desperate when he realizes he is trapped in a subway station, needing to complete a mission to get out.

verdict.reason: Take the trip for a lean thriller that knows exactly when to show its hand. Kawamura builds genuine dread in 95 minutes without wasting a single station stop.

ratings.plot: In an alternate present-day version of Oakland, telemarketer Cassius Green discovers a magical key to professional success, propelling him into a universe of greed.

verdict.reason: Riley's debut feature delivers one of the wildest third acts in recent memory, transforming workplace satire into full-blown surreal nightmare. The film's visual inventiveness and commitment to its own bizarre logic makes it essential big-screen viewing.

ratings.plot: A filmmaker forges an unusual friendship with an octopus living in a South African kelp forest, learning as the animal shares the mysteries of her world.

verdict.reason: The underwater cinematography is genuinely stunning, but this feels better suited for home viewing than a theatrical experience. The 85-minute runtime works in its favor, keeping the nature documentary focused without overstaying its welcome.

movies.title: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind

ratings.plot: When their relationship turns sour, a couple undergoes a medical procedure to have each other erased from their memories forever.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing that gets better with each theatrical experience. Gondry's practical effects and memory fragments work magic on the big screen in ways streaming cannot match.

ratings.plot: A middle-aged Chinese immigrant is swept up into an insane adventure in which she alone can save existence by exploring other universes and connecting with the lives she could have led.

verdict.reason: Buy the ticket for a film that demands theatrical immersion. The Daniels pack every frame with visual chaos that loses impact on smaller screens.

ratings.plot: A recently-widowed woman is on the road with her precocious young son, determined to make a new life for herself as a singer.

verdict.reason: See this underrated Scorsese while you can on film. Ellen Burstyn delivers one of the decade's finest performances in a rare screening of the director's most personal work.

ratings.plot: Two neighbors form a strong bond after both suspect extramarital activities of their spouses. However, they agree to keep their bond platonic so as not to commit similar wrongs.

verdict.reason: Worth the trip for Wong Kar-wai's most complete achievement. Christopher Doyle's cinematography creates a world of longing that demands the big screen.

ratings.plot: A nostalgic look at radio's golden age focusing on one ordinary family and the various performers in the medium.

verdict.reason: Charming but minor Woody Allen that works fine at home. The episodic structure and nostalgic tone play better as comfort viewing than essential cinema.

ratings.plot: A comedy (sort of) about young men in America.

verdict.reason: Promising debut worth supporting if you are curious about new voices. Boyson shows potential but the execution does not justify theatrical urgency.

ratings.plot: A New York woman apprentices for a dance company and throws herself headlong into her dreams, even as the possibility of realizing them dwindles.

verdict.reason: Greta Gerwig is magnetic but this plays just as well streaming. The black and white photography looks nice theatrically but the intimate scale suits smaller screens.

ratings.plot: A free-spirited woman "kidnaps" a yuppie for a weekend of adventure. But the fun quickly takes a dangerous turn when her ex-convict husband shows up.

verdict.reason: Demme at his most unhinged deserves the full theatrical experience. The tonal shifts from screwball to thriller work best when you cannot pause or look away.

ratings.plot: Documentary covering Bob Dylan's 1965 tour of England, which includes appearances by Joan Baez and Donovan.

verdict.reason: Essential documentary that captures Dylan at his peak creative arrogance. Pennebaker's cinema verite style pioneered the form and still feels electric in a theater.

ratings.plot: On the hottest day of the year on a street in the Bedford-Stuyvesant section of Brooklyn, everyone's hate and bigotry smolders and builds until it explodes into violence.

verdict.reason: Spike Lee's masterpiece burns brighter in communal viewing. The heat and tension build to a boiling point that streaming cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: Five teens visit a remote cabin and explore its cellar, finding strange items. When Dana reads from a book there, she unleashes zombie killers. But the situation is more complex than it appears.

verdict.reason: Clever horror deconstruction that works but is not urgent viewing. Goddard's meta-commentary plays fine at home and the scares lose punch on repeat viewings.

ratings.plot: On a faraway planet where blue giants rule, oppressed humanoids rebel against their machine-like leaders.

verdict.reason: Surreal animation masterpiece that demands big screen immersion. Laloux's alien landscapes and Topor's bizarre character designs create a world you need to get lost in.

ratings.plot: After getting out of prison, small-time crook Mardar stumbles upon a woman who looks exactly like his long-lost lover.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone serious about contemporary Chinese cinema or digital filmmaking as art form. Lou Ye's handheld DV camerawork turns Shanghai's polluted waterway into a fever dream of mirrors and doubles.

ratings.plot: A ship sent to investigate a wave of mysterious sinkings encounters the advanced submarine, the Nautilus, commanded by Captain Nemo.

verdict.reason: Disney's most sophisticated adventure film deserves the big screen treatment. The giant squid attack remains one of cinema's greatest practical effects sequences.

ratings.plot: A man boards a limousine to be driven to his day's work: nine mysterious "appointments."

verdict.reason: Carax's surreal vignette collection rewards patient art house devotees but tests general audiences. Denis Lavant's chameleonic performance across nine bizarre roles is the main draw.

ratings.plot: This contemporary romantic comedy based on a global bestseller follows native New Yorker Rachel Chu to Singapore to meet her boyfriend's family.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need the communal rom-com experience tonight. The Singapore wedding sequence is the only part that truly benefits from theatrical viewing.

ratings.plot: Two boys meet at an opera training school in Peking in 1924. Their resulting friendship will span nearly 70 years and endure some of the most troublesome times in China's history.

verdict.reason: Chen Kaige's epic demands theatrical immersion for its three-hour sweep through Chinese history. The Peking Opera sequences lose their power on smaller screens.

ratings.plot: An irresponsible and childish ex-con befriends a girl with cerebral palsy and develops a progressively stronger bond with her.

verdict.reason: Lee Chang-dong's brutal compassion requires your full attention in a darkened theater. Moon So-ri's fearless performance as a woman with cerebral palsy is impossible to forget.

ratings.plot: After finding an old rifle, a young boy joins the Soviet resistance movement against ruthless German forces and experiences the horrors of World War II.

verdict.reason: Klimov's war nightmare must be experienced theatrically for maximum impact. The sound design alone justifies leaving your apartment.

ratings.plot: The wife of the recently deceased lawyer tries to cope with grief after his loss and to keep his last case going in court.

verdict.reason: Kieslowski completists should make the trip, but casual viewers can wait. The film's political context about 1980s Poland requires more investment than his later masterworks.

ratings.plot: A lovelorn screenwriter becomes desperate as he tries and fails to adapt 'The Orchid Thief' by Susan Orlean for the screen.

verdict.reason: Jonze and Kaufman's meta-fiction about creative paralysis works best with an audience that gets the jokes. Nicolas Cage's dual performance as the neurotic Kaufman twins is career-peak work.

ratings.plot: Photographer Robert Kincaid wanders into the life of housewife Francesca Johnson for four days in the 1960s.

verdict.reason: Eastwood's mature romance plays better at home where the intimacy feels less exposed. Meryl Streep's Iowa housewife accent is the main reason to see it theatrically.

ratings.plot: New York actors rehearse Chekhov's "Uncle Vanya" in a dilapidated theatre.

verdict.reason: Malle's final film captures theatrical magic that streaming cannot replicate. Wallace Shawn and Andre Gregory's decade-long Chekhov workshop creates unrepeatable intimacy.

ratings.plot: Two melancholic Hong Kong policemen fall in love: one with a mysterious female underworld figure, the other with a beautiful and ethereal waitress at a late-night restaurant he frequents.

verdict.reason: Wong Kar-wai's neon-soaked Hong Kong romance belongs on the biggest screen possible. The handheld camera work and Faye Wong's convenience store dance sequence lose magic at home.

ratings.plot: A young girl discovers the truth about her family's seemingly spontaneous road trip in this poignant drama starring John Magaro (Past Lives).

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want to catch a promising new voice before the festival buzz fades. Magaro's presence elevates what could have been another indie road trip into something more grounded.

ratings.plot: In WWII Italy, a widow and her lonely daughter seek distance between themselves and the horrors of war.

verdict.reason: Essential De Sica that deserves the big screen treatment. Loren's powerhouse performance carries one of the most harrowing wartime stories ever filmed.

ratings.plot: A movie sound recordist accidentally records the evidence that proves that a car accident was actually murder and consequently finds himself in danger.

verdict.reason: Peak De Palma paranoia thriller that demands theatrical sound design. The audio layers and split-screen work lose impact on home viewing.

ratings.plot: A horror writer visits an Irish inn to scatter his parents' ashes, unaware the property is said to be haunted by a witch.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need a solid horror fix tonight. Mc Carthy delivers effective scares but nothing that elevates above competent genre work.

ratings.plot: After a young couple witnesses a gruesome highway accident, they soon realize they did not leave the crash scene alone, as a demonic presence called the Passenger won't stop until it claims them both.

verdict.reason: Decent possession horror but nothing urgent about catching it theatrically. Øvredal knows his craft but the scares work just as well at home.

ratings.plot: When his apartment building is torn down, a retired lifelong New Yorker goes on a cross-country odyssey with his beloved cat Tonto.

verdict.reason: Charming road movie that works better as comfort viewing than essential cinema. Carney's Oscar-winning turn is the main reason to seek it out.

ratings.plot: Follows the key people at an investment bank over a 24-hour period during the early stages of the 2008 financial crisis.

verdict.reason: Exceptional ensemble piece that makes financial collapse feel like a thriller. Chandor's debut remains the smartest film about the 2008 crisis.

ratings.plot: The relationship between four female temps all working for the same credit company is threatened with the arrival of a new hire, who lands a permanent position one of the women was vying for.

verdict.reason: Sharp office satire worth seeing for completists of 90s indie cinema. Sprecher's direction finds genuine melancholy in temp work tedium.

ratings.plot: A woman starts exhibiting increasingly disturbing behavior after asking her husband for a divorce. Suspicions of infidelity soon give way to something much more sinister.

verdict.reason: Zulawski's unhinged masterpiece demands big screen viewing for maximum impact. Adjani's performance is one of the most intense ever captured on film.

ratings.plot: An Arkansas waitress and a housewife shoot a rapist and take off in a '66 Thunderbird.

verdict.reason: Landmark feminist road movie that still packs a punch three decades later. Scott's direction turns the final act into genuine American mythology.

ratings.plot: John Wick is a former hitman grieving the loss of his true love. When his home is broken into, robbed, and his dog killed, he is forced to return to action to exact revenge.

verdict.reason: Go see what launched a franchise and redefined action choreography. Stahelski's stunt background creates the most elegant gunplay since John Woo.

ratings.plot: Following a man's suicide, time traverses back to reveal six chapters of his life on why he committed suicide.

verdict.reason: Lee Chang-dong's reverse chronology masterpiece rewards theatrical attention. The backwards structure creates devastating emotional impact that builds with each revelation.

ratings.plot: When a political decision is made as to the location of a new large chemical factory, Stefan Bednarz is put in charge of it. This honest communist party man has to confront the local community opposing the construction.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of cinema's greatest directors working in his early political mode. Kieslowski's rigorous examination of bureaucratic compromise predicts the moral complexity that would define his later masterpieces.

ratings.plot: Kwon returns to Seoul from the mountains and is given a packet of letters from Mori back from Japan to propose to her. Kwon drops and scatters the undated letters. She reads them and has to make sense of the chronology - and so mu...

verdict.reason: Essential Hong Sang-soo at his most playful, using scattered letters as both narrative device and metaphor for memory's disorder. The 66-minute runtime makes this one of his most accessible entry points without sacrificing the formal rigor that defines his best work.

ratings.plot: Two parallel stories about two identical women; one living in Poland, the other in France. They don't know each other, but their lives are nevertheless profoundly connected.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of cinema's most mysterious and beautiful films about connection across distance. Kieślowski's use of color and light creates images that linger long after the credits roll.

ratings.plot: Forced to leave his family in Sicily, Mimí finds a job in a factory in Turin, where he gets involved in an extramarital affair.

verdict.reason: Essential Wertmüller that holds up as both political satire and sexual comedy. Her signature blend of class warfare and bedroom farce works perfectly at feature length.

ratings.plot: A small-time reporter tries to convince the police that she saw a murder in the apartment across from hers.
Three lonely young denizens of Taipei unknowingly share an apartment used for sexual trysts.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of cinema's most uncompromising voices at his finest. Tsai Ming-liang's mastery of silence and space creates an urban loneliness so precise it becomes transcendent.

ratings.plot: A research team in Antarctica is hunted by a shape-shifting alien that assumes the appearance of its victims.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Carpenter's masterclass in paranoid tension and practical effects wizardry. Rob Bottin's grotesque creature work remains unmatched in horror cinema and demands the biggest screen possible.

ratings.plot: A wife questions her life choices as she travels to Stockholm to see her husband receive the Nobel Prize for Literature.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for Glenn Close's controlled performance, but the theatrical experience adds little to what streams just as well at home. Close delivers decades of suppressed rage through micro-expressions that work better in close-up than from theater seats.

ratings.plot: A watch salesman meets a young woman soon leaving for Paris and becomes infatuated, so he begins to change all the clocks in Taipei to Paris time.

verdict.reason: Essential Tsai Ming-liang at his most accessible, perfect for the big screen. The static long takes and minimal dialogue create a hypnotic rhythm that streaming cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: The combustible chemistry between a Polish florist and a British tourist in this charming postcard of sapphic synchronicity.

verdict.reason: A tight 71-minute runtime makes this sapphic romance an ideal theatrical experience that won't overstay its welcome. Ohs shoots with the intimacy of his mumblecore roots but adds visual poetry that rewards the big screen.

ratings.plot: Over the summer, a series of unfortunate happenings triggers a financial crisis for a young woman and she soon finds her life falling apart.

verdict.reason: Reichardt's masterclass in minimalist storytelling deserves the big screen treatment. Every gesture and glance carries weight in this perfectly calibrated 80-minute study of economic precarity.

ratings.plot: The ethereally beautiful Vicky recalls her romances with Hao Hao and Jack in the neon-lit clubs of Taipei.

verdict.reason: Essential Hou Hsiao-hsien that turns club culture into pure cinema poetry. The neon-soaked visuals and Shu Qi's magnetic performance demand the biggest screen you can find.

ratings.plot: In a corrupt, greed-fueled world, a powerful alchemist leads a messianic character and seven materialistic figures to the Holy Mountain, where they hope to achieve enlightenment.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Jodorowsky's most accessible surreal masterpiece. The alchemical imagery and practical effects create a hallucinatory experience that demands the big screen.

ratings.plot: The story of Dian Fossey, a scientist who came to Africa to study the vanishing mountain gorillas, and later fought to protect them.

verdict.reason: Weaver delivers one of her strongest performances in a film that earns its emotional weight through genuine commitment to its subject. Apted shoots the mountain sequences with a documentary immediacy that makes the gorilla encounters feel authentically dangerous and intimate.

ratings.plot: A mysterious blonde woman kills one of the patients of a psychiatrist and then goes after the high-class hooker who witnessed the murder.

verdict.reason: Peak De Palma sleaze that belongs on the big screen where his split-screen voyeurism and museum stalking sequence can work their queasy magic. The shower scene and elevator murder are textbook examples of how to build and release tension through pure cinema.

ratings.plot: Shallow, rich and socially successful Cher is at the top of her Beverly Hills high school's pecking scale. Seeing herself as a matchmaker, Cher first coaxes two teachers into dating each other.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Heckerling's razor-sharp dialogue and Silverstone's perfect performance as the paradoxically wise airhead. The film's wit holds up better than most 90s comedies and benefits from theatrical communal laughter.

ratings.plot: A successful, ego-maniacal architect who has spent a lifetime bullying his wife, employees and mistresses wants to make peace as his life approaches its final act.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for Demme devotees but the stage origins show in every static frame. The 130-minute runtime drains energy from what should crackle with Ibsen's psychological intensity.

ratings.plot: In pre-unified China, the King of Qin sends his concubine to a rival kingdom to produce an assassin for a political plot, but as the king's cruelty mounts she finds her loyalty faltering.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for Chen Kaige's visual mastery, but the nearly three-hour runtime demands serious commitment. The film's operatic scope and deliberate pacing work better when you're prepared for an epic historical meditation rather than expecting tight narrative momentum.

ratings.plot: On a dark, wet night a historic and regal Chinese cinema sees its final film. Together with a small handful of souls they bid "Goodbye, Dragon Inn".

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Tsai Ming-liang's meditation on cinema's death, best experienced in a theater where you become part of the ritual. The 82-minute runtime lets you sink into the hypnotic rhythms without testing your patience.

ratings.plot: A woman from the lower class desperately tries to get her daughter into the movies.

verdict.reason: Essential Visconti that deserves the big screen treatment. Anna Magnani delivers one of her most ferocious performances as a mother whose ambition borders on madness.

ratings.plot: Three hoodlums carefully case a small town while planning to rob the bank on the upcoming Saturday. On Saturday, things turn violent and deadly.

verdict.reason: Fleischer transforms a simple heist setup into a masterclass of tension and character study. The CinemaScope photography makes every frame feel claustrophobic despite the wide open desert setting.

Worth seeing if you're drawn to intimate character studies, but this feels more suited for home viewing than a theatrical experience. The 80% critical consensus suggests solid craftsmanship without the urgency that demands a trip to the cinema tonight.

movies.title: That Time I Got Reincarnated as a Slime the Movie: Tears of the Azure Sea

ratings.plot: Rimuru and friends visit Celestial Emperor Hermesia's resort island after the Jura-Tempest Federations opening ceremony. During their vacation, they encounter a mysterious woman, leading to a new incident by the azure sea.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you're already invested in Rimuru's world, but this feels like an extended TV episode. The theatrical experience adds little beyond bigger visuals for what amounts to vacation-themed filler between major story arcs.

ratings.plot: Jeff "The Dude" Lebowski, mistaken for a millionaire of the same name, seeks restitution for his ruined rug and enlists his bowling buddies to help get it.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for the Coens at their most quotable and casually brilliant. The film's cult status exists because every rewatch reveals new details in the meticulous production design and Roger Deakins' bowling alley cinematography.

ratings.plot: Wendy and her brothers are whisked away to the magical world of Neverland with the hero of their stories, Peter Pan.
ratings.director: Clyde Geronimi, Wilfred Jackson, Hamilton Luske

verdict.reason: Disney's golden age animation deserves the big screen treatment for its fluid character movement and rich Technicolor backgrounds. The flying sequences lose their magic on home viewing compared to theatrical projection.

ratings.plot: The story of martial-arts master Ip Man, the man who trained Bruce Lee.

verdict.reason: See this one in theaters for Wong Kar-Wai's singular take on martial arts cinema. The fight choreography unfolds like ballet in slow motion, turning every punch into pure visual poetry.

ratings.plot: An unexploded WWII bomb is discovered on a busy construction site in the centre of London. Chaos ensues as the military and police begin a mass evacuation against a ticking clock.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want tense procedural filmmaking, but the premise feels too thin for a full theatrical commitment. Mackenzie directs with his usual gritty precision, turning London into a pressure cooker of bureaucratic panic.

ratings.plot: Mark marries Marnie although she is a habitual thief and has serious psychological problems, and tries to help her confront and resolve them.

verdict.reason: One of Hitchcock's most psychologically complex films deserves the big screen treatment. The director's use of rear projection and color creates a deliberately artificial world that amplifies Marnie's fractured mental state.

ratings.plot: Follows Miranda Priestly's struggle against Emily Charlton, her former assistant turned rival executive, as they compete for advertising revenue amidst declining print media while Miranda nears retirement.

verdict.reason: A solid sequel that lacks the original's bite but delivers enough workplace comedy to justify the ticket price. Streep elevates familiar material with a more vulnerable take on Miranda, though the fashion industry satire feels less urgent twenty years later.

ratings.plot: High schooler Shiori's dream of online stardom turns into a mission to save the world from brainrot after an embarrassing video of her goes viral and she's trapped in her smartphone by a nefarious alter ego.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need fresh anime on the big screen tonight. Kawamori's mecha design expertise translates awkwardly to smartphone-trapped teenagers and viral video anxiety.

ratings.plot: A young actor's obsession with spying on a beautiful woman who lives nearby leads to a baffling series of events with drastic consequences.

verdict.reason: One of De Palma's most unhinged Hitchcock riffs deserves the big screen treatment. The telescope voyeurism and split-diopter shots work best when projected large enough to make you complicit in the protagonist's obsession.

ratings.plot: After hitting something with her car, a bourgeois Argentine woman's life slowly descends into paranoia and isolation, as she fears she may have killed someone.

verdict.reason: One of the essential films about guilt and class anxiety from Argentina's finest director. Martel's sound design creates a disorienting psychological landscape that only works in a theater.

ratings.plot: A middle-aged artist obsessed with his pretty young assistant, a precocious 12-year-old living in a hotel, and a neurotic lawyer with a possessive mother make up three Gotham tales.
ratings.director: Woody Allen, Francis Ford Coppola, Martin Scorsese

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for Scorsese's virtuoso opening segment alone, which ranks among his most dazzling short work. The anthology format lets you experience three master filmmakers working in miniature, even if Coppola's middle entry drags.

ratings.plot: The final sixty-seven days of Van Gogh's life are examined.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Pialat's uncompromising final portrait of artistic madness and the weight of creation. Jacques Dutronc delivers a magnetic performance that captures Van Gogh's fevered energy without romanticizing his suffering.

ratings.plot: A happily-engaged couple is put to the test when an unexpected turn sends their wedding week off the rails.

verdict.reason: Borgli delivers sharp relationship comedy but the premise wears thin over 105 minutes. His knack for awkward domestic situations saves what could have been standard wedding chaos.

ratings.plot: A divorced woman and her diabetic daughter take refuge in their newly-purchased house's safe room when three men break-in, searching for a missing fortune.

verdict.reason: Skip it unless you need a Fincher fix tonight. The director's technical precision elevates what amounts to a feature-length home invasion exercise.

ratings.plot: Centers on a temporary small-town sheriff who uncovers dark mysteries after a local bank robbery.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you need a tight crime thriller that gets in and out fast. Wheatley keeps the 91-minute runtime lean without his usual experimental detours.

ratings.plot: A young farm boy enlists to serve in World War I after his beloved horse is sold to the cavalry. His hopeful journey takes him out of England and onto the front lines as the war rages on.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need a cry on a big screen tonight. Spielberg's sentimentality works best when the horse carries the story between human segments, but the 146-minute runtime stretches thin across predictable wartime episodes.

ratings.plot: After a young man is murdered, his spirit stays behind to warn his lover of impending danger, with the help of a reluctant psychic.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want to experience Swayze and Moore's chemistry on a big screen, but the supernatural mechanics feel rickety compared to the romance. Goldberg's psychic performance carries the film's tonal shifts between earnest love story and silly spirit antics.

ratings.plot: A group of international passengers en route from Los Angeles to Shanghai are forced to make an emergency landing in shark-infested waters. Now they must work together in hopes to overcome the frenzy of sharks drawn to the wreckage.

verdict.reason: Harlin knows how to stage practical carnage but this high-concept survival premise works better as a guilty pleasure than essential viewing. The shark attacks deliver genuine tension when the film commits to its B-movie roots.

ratings.plot: A young beautician, newly arrived in a small Louisiana town, finds work at the local salon, where a small group of women share a close bond of friendship, and welcome her into the fold.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you need a reliable tearjerker with strong ensemble work. Julia Roberts gives her breakout performance before becoming a movie star.

ratings.plot: After his wife leaves, James quits his US career to care for his ailing mother with memory loss in his homeland. Their relationship grows hostile as he becomes her caregiver, leading to a journey of emotional healing.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want a serious family drama that earns its emotional moments. Vadikkal builds the caregiver tension through small, authentic details rather than manufactured conflict.

ratings.plot: Mathias, a virtuoso pianist, lives an impossible love story.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you connect with Desplechin's restless emotional style. The director's trademark mix of literary dialogue and handheld intimacy gives weight to what could have been another doomed musician romance.

ratings.plot: Long-buried wounds rise to the surface when iconic pop star Mother Mary reunites with her estranged best friend and former costume designer, Sam Anselm, on the eve of her comeback performance.

verdict.reason: Wait for streaming unless you need Lowery's latest right now. The director's trademark slow-burn approach works against the backstage drama setup that demands more urgency.

ratings.plot: A twist on the body swap genre.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want to catch Amanda Kramer's latest before it disappears from theaters. Her direction elevates familiar genre mechanics with the kind of visual precision that made Please Baby Please memorable.

ratings.plot: Two friends, while having drinks together, share their memories of visiting the seaside resort of Tongyeong.

verdict.reason: Essential Hong Sang-soo that rewards attention with its layered structure revealing how the same events look different through each friend's perspective. The director's signature long takes and naturalistic dialogue work perfectly in this deceptively simple setup about memory and truth.

ratings.plot: The definitive documentary on the New York School Painters. Featuring footage of all the major figures of the New York Art Scene between 1940-1970, showing many of the artists before they became famous.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for understanding the New York School and worth seeing these artists speak in their own words before fame changed everything. De Antonio captures Pollock, de Kooning, and Warhol with the unvarnished directness that made him the definitive documentary filmmaker of his generation.

ratings.plot: A toxic, ever-evolving alien life-form from the Dark Gaseous Nebula arrives to consume rampant pollution, and neither humanity nor Godzilla may be able to stop it.

verdict.reason: This is the strangest entry in the Godzilla series and worth seeing for its psychedelic weirdness alone. Banno turns kaiju destruction into an anti-pollution fever dream with animated sequences and nightclub musical numbers that feel completely unhinged.

ratings.plot: Behind the scenes of the roller derby circuit. A beautiful young woman joins a roller derby team, but her fierce independence and competitive spirit get her into trouble.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you're drawn to Vernon Zimmerman's oddball take on exploitation cinema. The roller derby setting gives it a distinctive grindhouse energy that most B-movies lack.

ratings.plot: When a boy learns that a beloved killer whale is to be killed by the aquarium owners, the boy risks everything to free the whale.

verdict.reason: Better watched at home where the family-friendly formula won't feel as thin on a big screen. The whale effects haven't aged well enough to justify the theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: A doctor washes ashore on an island inhabited by little people.
ratings.director: Dave Fleischer, Willard Bowsky, Orestes Calpini

verdict.reason: This Fleischer Studios feature deserves a big screen for its groundbreaking animation techniques. The rotoscoping and multiplane camera work still impress nearly a century later.

ratings.plot: Teenage Amy Harper, her boyfriend Buzz Dawson, and their friends Richie Atterbury and Liz Duncan visit a local carnival for a night of innocent amusement, but soon witness a fortune teller's murder and find that the exits are locked.

verdict.reason: Tobe Hooper's carnival horror belongs in a theater where the practical effects and Dean Cundey's cinematography can work their magic. The funhouse itself becomes a genuine maze of dread on the big screen.

ratings.plot: An American agent, under false suspicion of disloyalty, must discover and expose the real spy without the help of his organization.

verdict.reason: De Palma's split-screen compositions and the Langley vault sequence demand theatrical viewing. The practical stunts and paranoid atmosphere lose impact on smaller screens.

ratings.plot: A satirical fable following an animal revolution as pigs seize power and the farm descends into a ruthless dictatorship-fulfilling Orwell's warning about the dangers of communism.

verdict.reason: Wait for streaming where Serkis's motion-capture animation can be paused when the allegory becomes too heavy-handed. The runtime stretches thin for what should be a tighter adaptation.

ratings.plot: A well-planned robbery goes awry with tough cop Jim Cordell in pursuit of the thieves.

verdict.reason: Solid if you appreciate Richard Fleischer's efficient B-movie craftsmanship. The 67-minute runtime keeps the heist mechanics tight without overstaying its welcome.

ratings.plot: Jack and Judy are husband and wife, and Howard is Judys father. They live in some fictional undemocratic and repressive country, and tell us a story about their lives, mostly from Jack's point of view.

verdict.reason: David Hare's stage adaptation works better as theater than cinema. The static three-person monologue structure offers little that film can enhance.

ratings.plot: When a woman crashes at an empty Italian villa, posing as the owner's fiancée, she discovers an unexpected romance that could transform her life.

verdict.reason: Generic rom-com formula that streaming was invented for. The Italian scenery is the only draw, but not worth the ticket price for obvious plotting.

ratings.plot: What happened to the women at 10 Rillington Place? The story of British serial killer John Christie, who committed most or all of his crimes in the titular terraced house, and the miscarriage of justice involving Timothy Evans.

verdict.reason: Richard Attenborough's chilling performance as Christie benefits from the claustrophobic atmosphere a theater provides. Fleischer shoots the cramped London house like a psychological trap.

ratings.plot: After being fired for insubordination, homicide detective Mike Carter is hired as bodyguard by the owner of a local meat-packing plant where a meat inspector has been murdered.

verdict.reason: Fleischer's noir efficiency works if you want a tight 62-minute crime story. The meat-packing plant setting gives it a grittier edge than most B-noirs.

ratings.plot: She's the perfect all-American parent: a great cook and homemaker, a devoted recycler, and a woman who'll literally kill to keep her children happy.

verdict.reason: John Waters's suburban satire plays best with an audience that can laugh at Kathleen Turner's perfectly calibrated performance. The dark comedy timing needs the communal theater experience.

movies.title: Detective Bureau 2-3: Go to Hell Bastards!

ratings.plot: Police detective Tajima, tasked with tracking down stolen firearms, turns an underworld grudge into a blood-bath.

verdict.reason: Catch this rare Suzuki screening while you can. The director's wild yakuza style makes every shootout feel like a fever dream painted in neon.

ratings.plot: Cujo, a friendly St. Bernard, contracts rabies and conducts a reign of terror on an American farm.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need genuine tension on a big screen. Teague builds real dread in that car, but the film works just as well at home.

ratings.plot: A dysfunctional married couple retreats to a secluded cabin to repair their relationship, but each secretly plots to murder the other.

verdict.reason: Save your money for something sharper. Taccone never finds the right tone between the dark comedy and thriller elements.

ratings.plot: Blade forms an uneasy alliance with the vampire council in order to combat the Reapers, who are feeding on vampires.

verdict.reason: Del Toro transforms franchise vampires into something genuinely disturbing. The Reaper design alone demands the biggest screen you can find.

movies.title: Hello Mary Lou: Prom Night II

ratings.plot: Thirty years after her accidental death at her 1957 senior prom, the tortured spirit of prom queen Mary Lou Maloney returns to seek revenge.

verdict.reason: Worth it only if you love eighties practical effects. Pittman's ghost work is inventive, but the story never matches the craftsmanship.

ratings.plot: Two psychopathic young men take a family hostage in their cabin.

verdict.reason: The original Austrian version is superior and available streaming. Haneke's shot-for-shot English remake adds nothing to justify the theater trip.

ratings.plot: A ride-share driver is thrown into her worst nightmare when she picks up a stranger.

verdict.reason: Skip this generic thriller that wastes a solid setup. Prasad never builds real suspense beyond the obvious ride-share paranoia.

ratings.plot: Set in 1970, A BLIND BARGAIN reimagines the lost 1922 Lon Chaney silent film of the same name. A desperate young man strikes a dark deal with an unhinged doctor, offering his mother as a subject for the physician's twisted experim...

verdict.reason: Intriguing only as a curiosity about lost Chaney films. Bunnell's period recreation feels more academic than cinematic.

ratings.plot: Vice detectives Crockett and Tubbs, from Miami-Dade's organized crime bureau, go undercover as criminals to infiltrate and dismantle southern Florida's criminal enterprises.

verdict.reason: Kosinski's take lacks the visual style that made Mann's version work. The undercover plot feels tired without fresh directorial perspective.
Not enough information available to recommend spending tonight's ticket money. Wait for more details or streaming availability.

ratings.plot: A canine angel, Charlie, sneaks back to earth from heaven but ends up befriending an orphan girl who can speak to animals. In the process, Charlie learns that friendship is the most heavenly gift of all.
ratings.director: Don Bluth, Gary Goldman, Dan Kuenster

verdict.reason: Only worth it if you need Bluth animation on a big screen. The story gets heavy for family viewing, but the hand-drawn work still dazzles.

ratings.plot: After learning her multi-millionaire fiancé has already been married seven times, the daughter of a penniless marquis decides to tame him.

verdict.reason: Any Lubitsch deserves the theatrical treatment. The director's timing turns every marital power play into perfectly calibrated comedy.

ratings.plot: Returning from LV-426, Ellen Ripley crash-lands on the maximum-security prison Fiorina 161, where she discovers that she has unwittingly brought along an unwelcome visitor.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for Fincher's directorial debut despite studio interference. The Assembly Cut restores much of his vision and transforms a flawed theatrical release into something closer to his intended nightmare.

ratings.plot: Mario ventures into space, exploring cosmic worlds and tackling galactic challenges far from the familiar Mushroom Kingdom.
ratings.director: Aaron Horvath, Michael Jelenic, Pierre Leduc

verdict.reason: Another disposable Nintendo cash grab with nothing to justify the theater trip. The cosmic setting feels like a cheap excuse to recycle familiar beats from the first film.

ratings.plot: During a three-day heat wave just before a huge 4th of July celebration, an action star stricken with amnesia meets up with a porn star who is developing her own reality TV project, and a policeman who holds the key to a vast cons...

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in ambitious failure and prophetic satire. Kelly's bloated vision of America perfectly captures our current moment, making this the rare disaster that improves with time.

ratings.plot: The early life of the famous musician Michael Jackson, known as the King of Pop.

verdict.reason: Skip this sanitized biopic that treats Jackson like a product to be marketed. Fuqua's direction lacks the complexity needed to handle such a controversial figure.

ratings.plot: An 18-year-old female amateur dancer who performs nightly at a dancing bar, and works as a welder during the day, dreams of joining the Pittsburgh ballet school.

verdict.reason: Pure 80s artifice that works best as a time capsule experience. Lyne's music video aesthetics and the iconic water dance sequence deserve the big screen treatment.

movies.title: How to Stuff a Wild Bikini

ratings.plot: Frankie, on naval-reserve duty in Tahiti, doesn't trust Dee Dee to stay faithful, so he hires Bwana, a witch doctor, to help. Bwana conjures up a floating bikini, "stuffs" it with Cassandra, and sends her to distract advertising e...

verdict.reason: Bottom-tier beach party nonsense that wastes your evening. Even Asher's competent direction cannot salvage this witless entry in a played-out series.

ratings.plot: A comic book artist loses his hand, which in turn takes on a murderous life of its own.

verdict.reason: Stone's early horror work shows genuine psychological insight beneath the B-movie premise. Caine's committed performance elevates what could have been pure schlock into something genuinely unsettling.

ratings.plot: An honorable and mysterious rogue, known as Hanzala, makes himself an enemy of the Emperor Kisra after he helps a fugitive king and princess in the desert.

verdict.reason: Generic sword-and-sandal action that brings nothing new to the genre. Wyatt wastes the desert setting on poorly staged fight sequences and wooden dialogue.

ratings.plot: A mysterious young drifter who calls herself Nomi Malone hitches a ride to Las Vegas, where she finds work as a stripper and sets about clawing her way to the top of the Vegas showgirls.

verdict.reason: Verhoeven's masterpiece of American excess disguised as exploitation trash. The theatrical experience amplifies both the absurdity and the genuine critique of Vegas capitalism.

ratings.plot: Two high school outcasts find unexpected connection despite their personal demons. As Halsey battles addiction and Luke confronts his own darkness, their growing feelings force them to question if love is worth the risk.

verdict.reason: Skip this heavy-handed teen drama that mistakes darkness for depth. Méndez fails to find anything fresh in the troubled youth template.

ratings.plot: "Alice, an overworked woman trapped between a demeaning boss and an uncaring husband, discovers a mysterious pill called Elysium that makes everything seem perfect. In this distorted wonderland, the boundaries between real and ima...

verdict.reason: Worth catching if you appreciate experimental shorts that tackle modern anxiety. Powell's visual approach to the pill-popping premise shows real promise in a brief format.

movies.title: Carrie Mae Weems selects No Country for Old Men

verdict.reason: Essential viewing when curated by one of our greatest visual artists. Weems' selection transforms the Coen Brothers' masterpiece into a meditation on American violence and moral decay.

Worth it if you connect with Dean's meditative gallery film style. Her 16mm portraits of cinematic figures offer the kind of contemplative pacing that works better in a museum context than as evening entertainment.
Skip this forgotten melodrama unless you need to complete a filmography. The title alone signals the kind of dated gender politics that time has not been kind to.

ratings.plot: Two misfit best friends, Dar and Tuck, leave their dying coal-mining town with only one goal in mind - to reach sunny California and hook up with some beach babes. On the road, they meet gun-crazy outlaw Annie, and she takes over.

verdict.reason: Skip this road comedy knockoff that borrows Godard's title but none of his wit. Friedman's direction lacks the energy to make two misfits and a gun-crazy outlaw worth 82 minutes of your night.

ratings.plot: The madame of a high-class new York brothel teaches her younger sister all about the business.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you appreciate Metzger's sophisticated approach to adult cinema. His visual style elevates material that would be disposable in other hands, making this one of the classier entries in 70s erotica.

ratings.plot: A dark but comical glimpse at one day in the breakdown of a marriage.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you can handle Shawn's theatrical misanthropy for 90 minutes. Cairns stages the marriage breakdown with the kind of confined intensity that makes every exchange feel like a small knife wound.
See this Miyazaki masterpiece on a big screen while you can. The original Japanese version with subtitles preserves the vocal performances that give this environmental epic its proper weight.
Skip this adaptation of the infamous hoax novel unless you study exploitation cinema. The source material's satirical point about trashy bestsellers gets lost in a trashy film adaptation.
Worth it if you want to discover emerging voices in experimental cinema. Torres brings a fresh perspective to the kind of personal documentary work that benefits from theatrical focus.
See Aster's horror debut in a theater where the sound design can properly terrorize you. The film's domestic nightmare requires the kind of immersive experience that home viewing cannot match.

movies.title: A Bigger Splash + Mark Turbyfill by Markopoulos

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for the rare chance to see Markopoulos on film rather than video. His experimental portraits require the specific texture of projected celluloid to achieve their intended hypnotic effect.

movies.title: ACE Presents The Silence of the Lambs

verdict.reason: See Demme's thriller in a theater where Hopkins can properly unnerve a room full of people. The film's psychological intensity amplifies when shared with an audience that jumps at the same moments.

movies.title: Aloïse preceded by Qui donc a rêvé?

verdict.reason: Worth it if you have patience for Liliane de Kermadec's patient character study of the outsider artist. The pairing with the short film suggests this is curated for viewers ready to spend time with difficult subjects.

Worth seeing if you want Schnabel's artist biopic stripped of its colorful distraction. The black and white format forces focus on Bowie's Warhol and the downtown art scene dynamics rather than the paintings themselves.

Worth it if you want to see Cézanne's late paintings discussed in their historical context. Danièle Huillet and Jean-Marie Straub turn a museum lecture into rigorous cinema through their signature static shots and direct sound recording.

movies.title: Children of the Beehive + Children of War

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for these rare Shimizu prints that capture postwar Japanese devastation with unflinching clarity. The double bill showcases how Shimizu's neorealist approach influenced later masters like Oshima and Imamura.

Essential viewing for Shengelaia's hypnotic portrait of the Georgian painter, especially at Cinema Naïf's intimate setting. The film's static compositions mirror Pirosmani's own visual approach, creating an unusually direct connection between subject and cinematic form.

ratings.plot: Police gets a call-out to a lonely hotel in the Alps. When an officer gets to the hotel everything seems to be all right. Suddenly, an avalanche cuts them out from the rest of the world, and strange things are going to happen.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in Soviet-era genre filmmaking that predates Western sci-fi thrillers by years. Kromanov's atmospheric direction transforms a remote Alpine hotel into a genuinely unsettling psychological maze.

ratings.plot: A well-known politician woman receives a tape showing her son in a porno movie. She decides to hire a female detective to figure out who is trying to black-mail her.

verdict.reason: Skip this obscure Vecchiali oddity unless you need to complete a deep dive into French cinema's stranger corners. The blackmail-comedy premise never finds its footing across an overlong 86 minutes.

movies.title: Dream of Light preceded by Miró l'altre

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of Erice's greatest films about the act of seeing itself. The pairing with a Miró documentary creates a perfect double bill about artistic process and Spanish creative vision.

movies.title: Germany Year Zero preceded by Bambini in città

verdict.reason: Essential Rossellini double bill that pairs his devastating postwar masterpiece with a rare short film. The IFC Center screening offers a complete picture of the director's unflinching vision of children surviving urban devastation.

movies.title: Hiroshi Sugimoto selects The Face of Another

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for this rare pairing of Teshigahara's psychological masterwork with curation by one of Japan's greatest living artists. Sugimoto's eye for the uncanny makes him the perfect guide into this haunting study of identity and alienation.
Skip this one unless you're tracking every indie coming-of-age story. The execution feels more like a student film than a theatrical experience worth the trip.

movies.title: La Jetée preceded by How Not to be Seen

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Marker's 28-minute masterpiece that reinvented science fiction cinema through still photographs. The Monty Python short beforehand makes this double bill worth the trip to any venue programming real 16mm prints.

movies.title: Lee Friedlander selects North by Northwest

verdict.reason: Essential Hitchcock on a big screen, selected by one of America's greatest photographers. Friedlander's eye for American landscape makes this pairing perfect for experiencing Hitchcock's cross-country chase through iconic locations.
One of Chantal Akerman's essential films deserves the big screen treatment. The lengthy takes and Aurore Clément's restrained performance create a hypnotic rhythm that streaming cannot replicate.

movies.title: Muriel or the Time of Return

verdict.reason: Resnais at his most challenging rewards patient viewers with a theatrical experience. The fractured editing and overlapping dialogue create a puzzle that benefits from undivided attention.

movies.title: Nan Goldin selects The Naked Kiss

verdict.reason: Skip the Fuller cult worship and go for Goldin's curatorial eye instead. Her selection adds contemporary context to this 1964 melodrama about sex work and small-town hypocrisy.

movies.title: PRIVATE EVENT TODAY IN THEATER & COMMISSARY

verdict.reason: No public screening means no ticket to buy. Check back tomorrow for regular programming.

movies.title: The Mystery of Picasso + Rembrandt: Painter of Men

ratings.plot: Director Robert Zemeckis' deliciously twisted black comedy begins with Broadway has-been Meryl Streep bulldozing her way through a memorably misguided disco number and Goldie Hawn gorging herself on canned cake frosting – and things only get weirder and wilder from there. DEATH BECOMES HER offers up a classic catfight unlike anything you've ever seen, as terminally vain Madeline Ashton (Streep) clashes with her lifelong rival, beauty expert Helen Sharp (Hawn), in a perilous quest for eternal youth and glamour. Caught in the middle are plastic surgeon Ernest Menville (Bruce Willis) and the mysterious goddess-y Lisle Von Rhuman (Isabella Rossellini), who peddles an ancient elixir that provides "a touch of magic in this world obsessed with science." We've often heard "beauty is pain," and Madeline and Helen take that old adage to hair-raising new extremes. DEATH BECOMES HER deservedly won the best visual effects Oscar in 1993. But the eye-popping digital wizardry doesn't eclipse sensational comic performances from Streep, Hawn, and Willis, who create a hilariously offbeat love triangle under Zemeckis' stylish direction, and Alan Silvestri's urgently over-the-top score is the perfect frosting on this bittersweet cake. Yes, these are the moments that make life worth living...

verdict.reason: One of the great dark comedies about vanity and mortality, best experienced with a crowd that will gasp at every twisted visual effect. Zemeckis created digital wizardry that still looks disturbing thirty years later, and Streep and Hawn commit fully to the camp.

ratings.plot: MAMMA MIA! is the ultimate feel-good show that has audiences coming back again and again to relive the thrill. Now it’s your turn to have the time of your life at this smash-hit musical that combines ABBA’s greatest hits, including "Dancing Queen," "S.O.S.," "Super Trouper," "Take A Chance on Me," and "The Winner Takes It All," with an enchanting tale of love, laughter, and friendship. Whether it’s your first visit or your fourteenth, see the show that has the whole world coming back for more, because every time feels like the first time at MAMMA MIA! In the 2008 film adaptation, Pierce Brosnan, Colin Firth, Stellan Skarsgård, Christine Baranski, Julie Walters, Amanda Seyfried, and Dominic Cooper join Meryl Streep in this celebration of a mother, a daughter, and three possible dads. An independent, single mother who owns a small hotel on an idyllic Greek island, Donna (Streep) is about to let go of Sophie (Seyfried), the spirited daughter she's raised alone. For Sophie's wedding, Donna has invited her two lifelong best girlfriends – practical and no-nonsense Rosie (Julie Walters) and wealthy, multi-divorcee Tanya (Christine Baranski ) – from her one-time backing band, Donna and the Dynamos. But Sophie has secretly invited three guests of her own. On a quest to find the identity of her father to walk her down the aisle, she brings back three men from Donna's past to the Mediterranean paradise they visited 20 years earlier. Over 24 chaotic, magical hours, new love will bloom and old romances will be rekindled on this lush island full of possibilities.

verdict.reason: Pure theatrical joy that demands to be seen with other people who will sing along shamelessly. Pierce Brosnan's truly terrible singing voice becomes a feature, not a bug, when the whole theater is laughing together.

ratings.plot: RZA’S ONE SPOON OF CHOCOLATE follows Unique (Shameik Moore, DOPE and voice of Miles Morales in the Spider-Verse films), a veteran and ex-convict seeking a fresh start in a small town. Instead of waiting for his turn to be picked off, Unique and those closest to him (Paris Jackson, RJ Cyler) decide to fight back in this sharply satirical and stylish action thriller from the visionary leader of the Wu-Tang Clan.

ratings.plot: It’s the bubbly and hilarious legal comedy that taught us to never judge a book by its fabulous look, and more importantly, how to bend and snap. She decides the way back into his heart is to follow him to law school, but she soon finds something far more rewarding than her bonehead ex as she exceeds all the stuck-up expectations. Witherspoon shines as the tough but always effervescent Elle in this bleached-out comedy about not only proving your worth but doing it for yourself. Just remember to keep that perm dry. ratings.plot:
Selected by the Short Films Branch



For many years, author Stephen King had a “Dollar Baby Program” that would allow aspiring filmmakers to option his stories for adaptation for only one dollar. This event brings together several shorts created under the program: Paranoid (2000) is about two young women coping with an alcohol blackout; Disciples of the Crow (1983) explores a crow-worshipping cult whose members are children; Dedication (2021) tells of a Portuguese worker at a Berlin hotel and the lengths she will go to for her child; Popsy (2019) reveals a twisted gambling debt that leads to a child abduction; Beachworld (2019) portrays two astronauts struggling to survive on an alien world; and If You Tell Your Dreams... (2013) depicts a middle-aged couple and the memory of a... recent nightmare.



PROGRAM INCLUDES:



Paranoid DIRECTED BY: Jay Holben. WITH: Tonya Ivey. 2000. 8 min. USA. Color. Scope. English. Not Rated.



Dedication DIRECTED/WRITTEN BY: Selina Sondermann. WITH: Raquel Villar, Danielle Schneider, Buenaventura Braunstein, Carlos Lobo. 2021. 16 min. Germany. Color. English/German. Not Rated.



Popsy DIRECTED/WRITTEN BY: Jon Mann. WITH: Gary Belding, Kirk Belding, Charley Moore. 2019. 11 min. Canada. Color. English. Not Rated.



Beachworld DIRECTED/WRITTEN BY: Jackie Perez. WITH: Samantha Cutaran, Tom McCafferty, Lyn Ross. 2019. 14 min. USA. Color. Scope. English. Not Rated.



If You Tell Your Dreams... DIRECTED/WRITTEN BY: Yonatan Weinstein. WITH: Eli Danker, Ayelet Margalit. 2013. 7 min. Israel. Color. Hebrew. Not Rated.
ratings.director: Jay Holben, Selina Sondermann, Jon Mann, Jackie Perez, Yonatan Weinstein

verdict.reason: Skip past the expected horror beats and see rare adaptations that most King fans have never encountered. These six shorts from the author's low-budget program offer genuine discoveries spanning three decades of underground filmmaking.
